stem: A very large list of names has to be sorted alphabetically. A merge sort is used.
model_answer: A merge sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm. The list is repeatedly split in half until each sub-list contains only one item (a list of one is already sorted). Pairs of sub-lists are then merged back together: items from each are compared and placed into a new list in order. The merges continue until there is a single sorted list. Merge sort is much faster than bubble sort on large data, but uses extra memory for the temporary sub-lists.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Describe the two main stages of a merge sort.

mark_points.text: split stage: the list is divided in half repeatedly
continue splitting until each sub-list has one item (lists of one are already sorted)
accepted_alternatives: until each list has a single element

mark_points.text: merge stage: pairs of sub-lists are combined by comparing items and placing the smaller first
accepted_alternatives: merge by comparing front of each sub-list and picking the smaller

mark_points.text: merges continue until one fully sorted list remains
accepted_alternatives: keep merging until there is only one list left
